texto: If Canada could simply apply the basic principles of sustainable development, such as the internalization of costs and 'polluters pay,' it would have long-term beneficial effects, both environmental and economic.
explicacao: Orientação direta de Andrew Nikiforuk sobre temas ligados a saude mental. A afirmação “If Canada could simply apply the basic principles of sustainable development, such as the interna…” funciona como lembrete prático ligado a saude-mental, canada, development, útil em momentos de development.

frase_original: If Canada could simply apply the basic principles of sustainable development, such as the internalization of costs and 'polluters pay,' it would have long-term beneficial effects, both environmental and economic.

texto: Probably no single event highlights the strength of Campbell’s argument (on peak oil) better than the rapid development of the Alberta tar sands. Bitumen, the world’s ugliest and most expensive hydrocarbon, can never be a reasonable substitute for light oil due to its extreme capital, energy, and carbon intensity. Bitumen looks, smells, and behaves like asphalt; running an economy on it is akin to digging up our existing road infrastructure, melting it down, and enriching the goop with hydrogen until it becomes a sulfur-rich but marketable oil.
explicacao: Metáfora atribuída a Andrew Nikiforuk: compara elementos do cotidiano para falar de limites e escolhas conscientes. O trecho “Probably no single event highlights the strength of Campbell’s argument (on peak oil) better than…” usa imagem concreta para lembrar que reflexao, energy, global-warming pedem movimento e interpretação pessoal, em tom de viagem e global-warming.

frase_original: Probably no single event highlights the strength of Campbell’s argument (on peak oil) better than the rapid development of the Alberta tar sands. Bitumen, the world’s ugliest and most expensive hydrocarbon, can never be a reasonable substitute for light oil due to its extreme capital, energy, and carbon intensity. Bitumen looks, smells, and behaves like asphalt; running an economy on it is akin to digging up our existing road infrastructure, melting it down, and enriching the goop with hydrogen until it becomes a sulfur-rich but marketable oil.
